$metadata.purpose: Reference patterns for /plan and /design to draw from. NOT rigid templates — the LLM should adapt freely based on the brief, audience, and chosen richness.
$metadata.philosophy: Suggest, don't enforce. Skip recommendations that don't fit the specific brief. The aliases + decision rules help match a brief to an archetype; the richnessHints inspire what to add/skip; the LLM owns the final composition.
howToUse: 1. Match the brief to an archetype using `aliases` (case-insensitive substring match).
2. Read `richnessHints.narrative` for the design philosophy of this archetype.
3. Apply `decisionRules` to the specifics of this brief (audience, data volume, domain).
4. Use `richnessHints.considerForRicher` (or `considerForLeaner`) as inspiration based on the chosen richness — pick freely, don't checklist.
5. The chosen `recommendedShell` and `typicalSections` anchor the IA but can be overridden when the brief calls for something different.

typicalSections: KPI row (3-5 metrics) — Library health-style metric cards
primary content (table, chart, or master-detail pane) — the main activity
secondary content (activity feed, recent items, or detail pane) — context

richnessHints.narrative: What feels right depends on the audience. A monitor dashboard for execs leans summary-heavy with sparklines and trend lines. An ops dashboard for managers leans master-detail with a queue + active item pane. A status dashboard leans live-data with refresh affordances. A 'home' dashboard for end-users leans personalized + quick-actions.
considerForRicher: sidebar navigation with grouped sections (Workspace / Team / Account)
page header with Actions + Search + breadcrumbs + version chip
Featured icon + Actions + trend delta on Metric item cards
filter chip group above primary content (All / Pending / Approved / etc.)
master-detail pane (list left, active item right with details)
status badges with semantic color (success / warning / error / info)
activity feed in a right rail or as a section
+ New / primary CTA button in top right
right-side detail panel with Approve / Request changes actions
selected-state row highlight in lists
considerForLeaner: Disable Featured icon on metric cards
No filter chips — just a simple list

decisionRules: If audience is admin/manager AND data has reviewable items → master-detail
If primary content is a table > 50 rows → add filter chips + search
If the page is a status overview → add status badges with semantic color
If the domain involves comparisons (before/after, version diffs) → add a comparison preview pane
If the page tracks 'pending action items' → add a primary CTA button (e.g., '+ New review')
If the audience needs to make decisions (approve/reject) → add action buttons in a detail panel

typicalSections: left sidebar nav (~240px) — primary IA
middle list pane (~400-500px) — items to review with filters at top
right detail pane (~fill) — selected item with full context + actions

richnessHints.narrative: The user's job is to work through a queue of items, looking at one at a time. The list shows scannable summaries; the detail pane shows everything. Critical: the selected item must be visually distinct in the list, and the detail pane must always reflect the current selection.
considerForRicher: filter + sort controls above the list
search input above the list
selected-state row highlight (left border accent + bg fill)
status badges on each list row
metadata row per list item (author + timestamp + count)
tabbed sections in the detail pane (Overview / Comments / History)
threaded comments thread in detail pane
primary action buttons fixed at the bottom of detail pane (Approve / Request changes)
before/after comparison preview if items represent changes
considerForLeaner: no filters, just a chronological list
actions inline per row instead of a detail pane

decisionRules: If list items represent design/code changes → add before/after preview in detail pane
If items are reviewable → add Approve + Request changes buttons in detail pane
If items have discussion → add threaded comments in detail pane
If list > 20 items → add filter chips for status
If list > 50 items → add search input

typicalSections: left sidebar (~240px) — identity + nav
center feed (~620px) — composer at top, then post cards
right rail (~300px) — sponsored / suggestions / contacts

richnessHints.narrative: The feed is the reason the user opened the app. Composer at the top invites action. Post cards stack vertically with consistent rhythm. Right rail is supplementary — never compete with the feed for attention.
considerForRicher: stories carousel at top of feed
post composer with media affordances (Photo / Video / Live)
engagement actions on each post (like, comment, share)
embedded reposts inside post cards
right-rail sections: sponsored, birthdays, online contacts

decisionRules: If platform supports media → composer should expose Photo + Video buttons
If platform has social graph → add right-rail contacts/birthdays
If platform monetizes → add sponsored section above organic content (clearly labeled)

typicalSections: left sidebar (~240px) — section nav (Account / Notifications / Billing / etc.)
main content (~fill) — form sections with labels + inputs
save bar at bottom (sticky) — Save changes / Cancel

richnessHints.narrative: Settings pages need to be scannable AND editable. Group related fields under section headers. Show current values clearly. Disable Save until changes exist; surface validation inline. The save bar prevents lost work.
considerForRicher: section headers with description text per group
inline validation on each field
helper text under inputs explaining choices
toggle rows for boolean settings (with description)
danger zone at bottom (delete account, etc.) visually separated
sticky save bar with Cancel + Save
breadcrumbs in page header showing Account > Notifications
considerForLeaner: no save bar, autosave per field
no danger zone (or move to a separate page)

decisionRules: If save is explicit (not autosave) → sticky save bar required
If page edits a destructive setting → add danger zone with confirmation
If multiple sub-sections → use sidebar nav, not tabs

typicalSections: hero copy on left (~560px) — brand wordmark + value prop
auth form card on right (~396px) — email + password + CTAs

richnessHints.narrative: Auth landing pages have one job: get the user in or signed up. Minimize cognitive load. Big primary CTA. Clear secondary path (Create new account). 'Empty' state isn't meaningful here — this IS the first-time view. States that matter: Filled (with credentials) and Error (invalid credentials).
considerForRicher: social SSO buttons (Google / Apple / GitHub)
'Remember me' checkbox
trust badges or social proof below the form
language picker in footer
marketing illustration on the hero side

decisionRules: If product supports SSO → add social buttons above email/password divider
If audience is global → add language picker in footer
States to build: Filled + Error (NOT Empty + Error — Empty is ill-defined for auth)

typicalSections: top navigation with logo + product/pricing/login + primary CTA
hero section with headline + subhead + CTA + visual
social proof (logos / quote / metric)
feature sections (3-6 features)
secondary CTA section
footer with sitemap + legal

richnessHints.narrative: Marketing pages are persuasion engines. Hierarchy is everything: hook → proof → features → CTA. Every section earns its place. Avoid the 'wall of features' — group meaningfully. Visuals carry as much weight as copy.
considerForRicher: press logo strip below hero
testimonial section with avatars + quotes
metric callouts (10M users, 99.9% uptime)
feature sections with screenshots/illustrations
comparison table (us vs competitors)
FAQ section above footer
CTA banner before footer with secondary signup ask

decisionRules: If product has notable customers → press logo strip + testimonials
If product is in a crowded space → comparison table
If audience asks the same questions repeatedly → FAQ section
If product has measurable scale → metric callouts in hero or below
